serving.natural_language_portion: One regular cup of brewed plain black tea, approximately 500 ml, as typically served in a Singapore coffeeshop or tea house.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water (FDC ID 173228)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain brewed black tea prepared with tap water. Values are per 100 g (100 ml) of brewed beverage. This is the closest approved-source match for plain unsweetened brewed black tea. The input record's values (energy 1 kcal, protein 0.1 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 0.2 g, sodium 1 mg) are consistent with USDA data and have been adopted with minor rounding alignment.

gi_source.title: Plain brewed black tea — glycemic index not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content (0.2 g/100 ml); University of Sydney GI Database consulted
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain unsweetened brewed black tea contains only 0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 ml and 1.0 g per 500 ml serving. This quantity of carbohydrate is insufficient to produce a measurable glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for plain black tea because the carbohydrate content is too low to test. glycemic_index is set to 0 (numeric sentinel for not-applicable low-carbohydrate beverage) and gi_category to not_applicable. Glycemic load per 500 ml serving is 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (1 mg/100 ml), zero sugar, zero fat; moderate caffeine content
hypertension.guidance: Plain unsweetened black tea is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content (1 mg/100 ml) and absence of sugar and fat; however, consumption should be limited to 3–4 cups per day to avoid caffeine-mediated acute blood pressure elevation, and no sugar, condensed milk, or honey should be added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero sugar, negligible carbohydrates (0.2 g/100 ml), no glycemic impact; theaflavins may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain unsweetened black tea has zero sugar and only 0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 ml, producing no measurable glycemic response, and epidemiological evidence suggests regular consumption of black tea theaflavins and catechins may modestly improve insulin sensitivity; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume it without added sugar or sweeteners.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, zero sodium, rich in theaflavins and thearubigins with antioxidant and endothelial-protective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain unsweetened black tea contains no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and negligible sodium, and its theaflavin and thearubigin polyphenols have been associated with improved endothelial function and modest LDL-cholesterol reduction in clinical trials; it is appropriate for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or cream.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Theaflavins and thearubigins with documented anti-inflammatory polyphenol activity; zero pro-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain black tea contains theaflavins and thearubigins with documented anti-inflammatory properties and no pro-inflammatory sugar, fat, or sodium; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume it freely but should avoid adding sugar or cream, and should consume it between meals rather than with iron-rich foods due to tannin-mediated iron absorption inhibition.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Tea (凤眉红茶, Hóng Chá) is a plain unsweetened brewed black tea, specifically a Fengmei-style Chinese black tea, served as a beverage in Singapore and across Chinese-influenced tea culture in Southeast Asia. It is prepared by steeping fully oxidised black tea leaves — in this case the Fengmei (凤眉, 'phoenix eyebrow') grade — in hot water, producing a clear amber-to-reddish-brown liquor with a mellow, slightly sweet, and clean finish characteristic of heavily roasted Chinese black teas. The beverage contains no milk, sugar, or additives in its plain form. Nutritionally, plain brewed black tea is extremely low in calories (approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml), contains negligible protein (0.1 g/100 ml), zero fat, and minimal carbohydrates (0.2 g/100 ml), making it essentially a non-caloric beverage. It is naturally free of sugar, saturated fat, cholesterol, and dietary fibre. Its sodium content is negligible at 1 mg per 100 ml. Black tea is a meaningful source of polyphenols — specifically theaflavins and thearubigins formed during the oxidation process — which have been associated with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cardioprotective effects in clinical and epidemiological research. Potassium content is approximately 37 mg per 100 ml. The beverage is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or dairy. It is halal, vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free. A standard serving in Singapore is approximately 500 ml (one regular cup). The glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content.
common_user_queries: Is black tea good for diabetes?
How many calories in black tea?
Is black tea suitable for hypertension?
What are the health benefits of black tea?
How much caffeine is in black tea?
Is black tea good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values for plain brewed black tea are well-established in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, FDC ID 173228) and are consistent with the input record values. The food is a simple single-ingredient brewed beverage with minimal preparation variability. All values are supported by an approved primary source.
notes: fat_g is naturally zero: plain brewed black tea contains no fat by composition, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173228. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all naturally zero for the same reason. sugar_g is naturally zero: plain unsweetened black tea contains no added or intrinsic sugar, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero: brewed tea liquid contains no dietary fibre as the leaf solids are removed during brewing, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero: plant-derived beverage contains no cholesterol. glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value indicating the GI test is not applicable; gi_category is set to not_applicable because the carbohydrate content (0.2 g/100 ml, 1.0 g per 500 ml serving) is insufficient to produce a measurable glycemic response; the University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for plain black tea. Potassium (37 mg/100 ml), folate (5 mcg DFE/100 ml), riboflavin (0.01 mg/100 ml), iron (0.02 mg/100 ml), magnesium (3 mg/100 ml), zinc (0.02 mg/100 ml), and phosphorus (1 mg/100 ml) values sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 173228. The Fengmei (凤眉) designation refers to a specific grade of Chinese black tea from Fujian province; nutritionally it is equivalent to standard brewed black tea. The ethnic_tag 'Milk Tea' in the input record is not applicable to this plain unsweetened preparation and has been excluded from the output classification. Portion size of 500 g (500 ml) retained from input as a typical Singapore coffeeshop serving size.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: G00011818 Black Tea (凤眉红茶) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The record uses USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173228) as the approved nutrient source. All zero macro values are justified in notes. glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 with gi_category not_applicable, resolving the iteration-2 validator failure that required a numeric value. Multilingual naming satisfies the Singaporean-dish requirement (English, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). All three required health conditions are present with clinical guidance and correct human_condition_name formatting. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent between aliases_by_language and the flat aliases array. Ontology anchors are defensible and not invented.
